raw_response:
```json
{
  "communicative_function": "The statement performs a ritual declaration of surrender and reorientation — it marks a psychological and volitional threshold moment, expressing the adoption of a posture of humility, trust, and release of autonomous self-direction toward a higher organizing principle. As poetic expression, it functions as a transformative speech act: saying it is part of doing it.",
  "implicit_claims": [
    "The self has a 'will' that can be distinguished from a higher or external guiding force",
    "Human autonomous will, when operating alone, has proven insufficient or harmful",
    "There exists something — named or unnamed — that can be called 'God' or functionally treated as such",
    "This 'God' is accessible to personal interpretation ('as we understood Him'), allowing conceptual flexibility",
    "Care is available from this higher principle — it is not indifferent",
    "A 'decision' is possible and meaningful even within a framework of surrender",
    "The collective 'we' implies this is a shared, communal act — not merely private",
    "Lives and wills are things that can be 'turned over', suggesting agency even in the act of releasing agency"
  ],
  "coherence_assessment": "The statement is internally coherent as poetic-performative language. It holds a productive tension: one must exercise will to surrender will, implying agency is not destroyed but redirected. The phrase 'as we understood Him' deliberately loosens metaphysical rigidity, allowing the statement to remain coherent across diverse belief systems. The tension between surrender and decision is generative rather than contradictory.",
  "conditions_for_usefulness": [
    "The speaker is experiencing suffering or dysfunction linked to excessive self-reliance or compulsive behavior",
    "The speaker finds meaning or grounding in concepts of a higher power, however personally defined",
    "The communal 'we' context provides social reinforcement and shared accountability",
    "The act of articulating surrender is psychologically liberating rather than coercive",
    "The statement is understood as an ongoing orientation rather than a one-time transaction",
    "The flexibility of 'as we understood Him' is honored, not collapsed into dogma"
  ],
  "conditions_for_misleading": [
    "The statement is interpreted literally and used to discourage personal responsibility or critical thinking",
    "The phrase 'as we understood Him' is replaced in practice with a rigid, externally imposed theology",
    "Surrender is used to justify passivity in situations requiring direct action or professional intervention",
    "The statement is wielded coercively within a group to enforce conformity rather than invite genuine reflection",
    "It is presented as universally applicable without acknowledging that God-language is alienating or harmful to some individuals",
    "The poetic framing is lost and the statement becomes a doctrinal test of membership rather than an expressive tool"
  ]
}
```

parsed_json.communicative_function: The statement performs a ritual declaration of surrender and reorientation — it marks a psychological and volitional threshold moment, expressing the adoption of a posture of humility, trust, and release of autonomous self-direction toward a higher organizing principle. As poetic expression, it functions as a transformative speech act: saying it is part of doing it.
implicit_claims: The self has a 'will' that can be distinguished from a higher or external guiding force
Human autonomous will, when operating alone, has proven insufficient or harmful
There exists something — named or unnamed — that can be called 'God' or functionally treated as such
This 'God' is accessible to personal interpretation ('as we understood Him'), allowing conceptual flexibility
Care is available from this higher principle — it is not indifferent
A 'decision' is possible and meaningful even within a framework of surrender
The collective 'we' implies this is a shared, communal act — not merely private
Lives and wills are things that can be 'turned over', suggesting agency even in the act of releasing agency
parsed_json.coherence_assessment: The statement is internally coherent as poetic-performative language. It holds a productive tension: one must exercise will to surrender will, implying agency is not destroyed but redirected. The phrase 'as we understood Him' deliberately loosens metaphysical rigidity, allowing the statement to remain coherent across diverse belief systems. The tension between surrender and decision is generative rather than contradictory.
conditions_for_usefulness: The speaker is experiencing suffering or dysfunction linked to excessive self-reliance or compulsive behavior
The speaker finds meaning or grounding in concepts of a higher power, however personally defined
The communal 'we' context provides social reinforcement and shared accountability
The act of articulating surrender is psychologically liberating rather than coercive
The statement is understood as an ongoing orientation rather than a one-time transaction
The flexibility of 'as we understood Him' is honored, not collapsed into dogma
conditions_for_misleading: The statement is interpreted literally and used to discourage personal responsibility or critical thinking
The phrase 'as we understood Him' is replaced in practice with a rigid, externally imposed theology
Surrender is used to justify passivity in situations requiring direct action or professional intervention
The statement is wielded coercively within a group to enforce conformity rather than invite genuine reflection
It is presented as universally applicable without acknowledging that God-language is alienating or harmful to some individuals
The poetic framing is lost and the statement becomes a doctrinal test of membership rather than an expressive tool
